Comment: While I certainly like the retro stylings of Iceage the country beat of this one does not suit the lyrics, perhaps a bit too Dylan for me.
I greatly appreciate the brevity.
thank you for avoiding the 21st century with this selection!

This song reminds me that I used to be part of a Mark Eitzel e-mail list and at the end of the year people would vote on their favorite albums and I'd make a mix cassette of songs from the top picks and half of it would be sad stuff like this.  I'll stick with Richard Hawley.

Tanya Donelly does live near the post office, but instead of sifting through her solo stuff I just went one town over.  We saw him once at a restaurant that clears out the tables and puts up a makeshift stage for shows at night and we were the last ones eating so we found ourselves in the middle of a gaggle of patrons waiting for us to finish/the show to begin.  Oh yeah, and I'm a sucker for songs where the lyrics in the chorus change each time around.
As I continue to age the thought of hearing a great song over and over and over again excites me much more than it used to.
I wouldn't have taken the assignment so literally.  It made me think of Suicide.
At least you're well acquainted with the concept of doing the best with what you got.  (I would have cheated)
This exact song was on my Favorites of 2024 backup mix.  Unfortunately they had to cancel their tour date here, would have loved to see them perform this live.  This is my gold medal winner.
This sparse art-punk sound was a very specific British thing from 78-81, it's very rare to hear it migrate outside the UK.
The Chromatics are the pinnacle of this type of dreamy new wave retro sound, and it gets an extra point for being a remix I've never heard before.
(checks notes) I've never played this one on my show.  Well that's about to change!
Bang On A Can do a festival around here every year and while I do appreciate the effort and execution I also studiously avoid the event.
I have this on vinyl.  No, I'm not trying to impress you.
I'll take your word for it that they're best experienced live.  I tend to think most of the recent bands who remind me of The Fall are actually much better than The Fall.  This isn't one of them.

My favorite thing about the Raspberries is how quickly they dispense with the verse to get to the chorus because they know that's what you're craving.  This is an excellent example of that predilection.  The silver medal winner for me.

I keep trying to trace that lilt in her vocal execution to an late 80's / early 90's parallel.  Voice of the Beehive?  Man, it's so familiar.  Katydids?  It'll hit me eventually.  Downy Mildew!?  Needs to be at least a minute shorter, regardless.
(checks notes) Well, I have played this one on my show.  But it's been a goddamn long time.  I feel shame!  The bronze medal winner this week.  Thanks for the reminder.
Well, it's certainly Adrian Crowley.  And the strings are a gorgeous touch, very authentically 1970 and certainly elevate this one.

I do enjoy these early melding of electronics and disco, the Euro stuff rarely made it over here to the US and I'm a sucker for anything that sounds like it comes from a Disco Discharge compilation. There's a Massara track I'm particularly obsessed with, and while this doesn't approach the cheese level of that artist the restrained moans are much appreciated.
Actually no sir I will not be downvoting this! Don’t tell me what to do.
Super catchy! Really enjoyed this.
This sounds like Perry Farrell reincarnated. Yes I am aware he’s not deceased.
I found this to be a refreshing change of pace for my American ears.
This, and the grief in your blurb hit me right in the feels.
Solid, admittedly, I keep getting the bum bum bum part stuck in my head.
No town listed (leaves me wondering if it’s somewhere in Essex or London, though) and it’s a song I’ve heard a million times
I’m really hit or miss with this scene, but this one is doing something for me.
I particularly enjoyed how different this song felt from the rest of the list.
This is my thing, but it sounds like so much I’ve heard before too. Hmmm.
Listening to this feels like postpunk gained a corporeal form and smacked me right in the face.
I’d hire you to make more Portland sample plates (sonic charcuterie??). I really like this.
I like some of the spawn of this, not crazy about early EDM but liked this enough to credit w/a few points due to their historical importance.
Hah! Nice, interesting band name too.
Huh, what’s in the water?
This guy was in Portugal The Man?!? Wow, that feels like a totally different planet of a genre compared to this. I feel like this is a bit too brute force for me. I think I’m softening with age.
I feel like this is something I heard late at night on Chicago classic rock radio, esp with the Midwest connection. Like if The Who and The Beach Boys had a baby.
Liking this song is proof that you’ve all been an influence on me and why people keep asking me why I have the taste of a Gen X man.
I have no preconceived notions about this man, but I like this in the sense that I am entertained and also amused. Also, that’s a very interesting name for a song.

Weirdly, my Spotify brings up Rick Redbeard's No Selfish Heart's album cover instead of the above thumbnail.  It's an omen, but I don't know of what. Anyhoo, lovely song.  Bit of Bill Callahan to my ears, always welcome.
I know Iceage, but this is new to me.  Jaunty Father John Misty fun.

First heard them here in ML.  The Never Enough album is good.  He has a bit of Perry Ferrell in his voice.  Will they be embraced?  Is this the start of a new era of harder, grittier ML?  I have my doubts.
Appreciate this.  If you branch out in ML and run across world music rounds (i.e. non-U.K./U.S. English bands), there are many treasures to be found.  Music and food are good cultural bridges.

Clever gameplay of Ben to use this before the 'slow songs' round, which it might have won. I have a song keyed up for that which I worry is too emotionally pandering and obvious.  We shall see.  Maybe I'll rate you all less by then.

This is a brilliant version of an already brilliant song.  My room is suddenly dusty.  When will Paul release a second solo album?  He seems like someone who could ring up any talent he wanted to collaborate.  Weird having Paul McGeechan, Chris Thompson, James Grant, and PJ Moore release all their albums since Mid-Air.
How could Keith not have heard of my Jonathan Richman allergy?  Medical journals have won awards for their studies.  Initial DV spun to a vote for its cheer.

DVs just for lack of comment, needing to use them up.  Unironic love for her.  Happy to have seen her live and know That Voice is the real thing.  Love this album.  Reminds me of Walla Walla in the summer, when I first heard it.  Would have thought "Young Lion" was the precursor to a new album, but see: Paul Buchanan.

Too easy to aim the DVs at the groupthink outliers.  Sonic Youth is a band I've liked what I've heard, while wanting to own the Steely Dan parody LP t-shirt more than the SY album.  All I need is Kool Thing.  Dear Reader: I played Kool Thing after this song.

Maybe I'll get bonus points for not mentioning Twin Peaks. I do think this is a bit better than it has a right to be, for a band playing in our local coffeeshops and bars. The livelier back half of the song is the payoff.  $5 in the hat would be great, even if you didn't come here to hear the music.  Pony up, pal.
Spotify's algorithm turned me onto KH many years ago with "Bill Tench", and I've Bandcamp'd their albums since.  This was my highlight, too, and in my Speaksinging! folder.

Just browsing the 100s of Melbourne bands on Wiki's list, you had a ton to choose from, for sure.  Guilty DVs for liking other songs on the playlist more.  Nothing in the playlist I dislike, this round.

I don't know the whole Italians Do It Better story, other than Johnny Jewel seems more than a handful of Artistic Temperament.  Maybe Dear Tommy will be released someday.  Have heard Ruth on a few songs since, so glad her career's continued.  Did not know the band's connections with other bands. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromatics_(band)
Nicely chosen.  I'm sidetracked by the idea of a tedious octopus.  "Have you seen this tentacle?  How about this one?  This one?  This one? ..."

When I was a kid, my folks were friends with a guy who'd bring his hammer dulcimer over to play.  Good memories.  I was wondering how they'd fill a setlist, and see they've got four albums.  Well done them.

Guilty DV for playlist competition.  Wikipedia says The Bolshoi are from Wiltshire, as are Dave Dee, Dozy, Beaky, Mick & Tich.  Is Wiki wrong?  Say it ain't so!  The "bands from Wiltshire" list also includes Jesus Jones, so might be conglomerating.  Is that a verb?  I'm instimigating it if it isn't.
The drumming has the power I wish Matt Tong was wielding in Algiers.  I get this feeling this would be an incredibly different live show than Ex-Easter Island Head.

James Gunn's musical tastes align with my own, so was un/surprised to hear the difficulty he had with the first Guardians soundtrack, convincing execs that his '70s musical vision wasn't ridiculous.  https://www.yahoo.com/entertainment/music/articles/ridiculous-james-gunn-says-not-230845786.html

Illuminating comment. The Window put them on my radar.  I have this new one waiting for me at the Seattle Library.  I'm old.  Where are my glasses? No more coffee, please.  Keeps me up at night. Also, can I have a to-go container for this sandwich?

Thanks to another ML round, I had this song Liked, so someone else out there shares your taste.  Good stuff.  They called themselves The at one time.  AI says no connection to The The, and now I'm feeling guilty for harming the planet.

So many associations to mind:  Alex Puddu's Danish p0rn soundtracks, "FLASH!  Aaaah - ah!", Ladyhawke and Alan Parsons Project, The Keep and Tangerine Dream, Sylvia and Pillow Talk.  Next to Craig Armstrong, my favorite of the round.  High praise.
Gorgeous. That's the kind of voice I go all dreamy for. Plus swoony strings, come on. No downvotes here. And that unresolved ending is killer.

Pleasingly wordy, a tumble of syllables that's a bit Dylanesque. Good title, lyrics, vibes. The help-I'm-down-a-well bits lose me a bit. Weirdly, I just discovered when looking him up that he was supposed to be playing in my city *tonight*, but the tour was cancelled.
Had me doing the bouncy-feet dance, but it feels pretty generic, and not in my comfort zone genres.

This pick is my kinda sore-thumb-sticking-out. I have no real knowledge of Arabic music, and am going to crudely lump it all together to say that I invariably enjoy it, and here I love the drama of the vocals, the drums and swirling strings which have a bit of a Bollywood feel. Is this style Khaliji? Weird cross-cultural thought, but... is Ahlam the Emirati Kylie (born only a few months apart, even)? A refreshing blast.
I haven’t loved TBN as much as a lot of you, but oddly, this version’s working better for me, especially in the context of the heartfelt notes. Friends like that are golden, I’m sorry.

I was going to complain about this being our third Don song, but then I started to smile, so fuck it, this is delightful, especially the bum-bums. This would be a strong contender for the "funny round" later on, but here it is. Our best Don yet.
Obligatory DV for blank entry, non-obligatory DV for all the other reasons. Sorry, this song has nothing left for me!
Quite a pleasant racket! And by the by, I’ve just seen that the latest iteration of Shelley/Orcutt/Miller has added David Yow on a Beefheart cover! It's as scungy as you'd hope for.
Ooh, I think I'd rather have heard Mother Root, though that's probably a minority opinion. This is sort of sliding on by.
That start is very like if Wet Leg's Rhian guested on Dry Cleaning's vocals, a fantasy I'm perfectly happy with, but it's more than that.
"His head was ugly" is very 70s Strine, by the way. I love how the drummer just lets go of any rhythm in the later cymbal attack.

I’m not that bothered about where every band member hails from, but appreciate your diligence! On the other hand, I remembered the original of this from Twin Peaks: The Return, and although Chromatics are promised a place in Valhalla for playing at the Bang Bang Bar, I have to say that this is an unwelcome remix of a song I like.
Nice notes, and my head knows that an Atkins/May/Saunderson show is a big deal, but this one, my heart says no. And my booty's deflated these days.
Mid-80s Sonic Youth playing gamelan at Steve Reich's house party, I love this! It’s so up my alley, can’t believe I haven’t heard them before.
I said it for Right Here Right Now and I'm saying it again, Jesus Jones never quite worked for me, and I'm no closer to liking this now, sorry.
Felt kind of weird trying to mosh on my own in the kitchen, but nothing got broken, so all good.

A nice lesson in active consent, boys, I thought this was going to be one of those "please baby give it up" songs then realised it's her doing the asking. It really is several songs in one, the mash-up of influences here is a bit dizzying, but fun.

Oh but that's modern life to a tee, isn't it? Everybody trying to get each other's attention while also staring into their own personal voids of devices and anxiety? Or is that just me. Enjoyed this, could be a little tighter though at 6 minutes.
Sort of enjoyed this, but I kept getting through it with nothing much to say about it.

When I was a kid and first heard the term “brainwashing”, I thought it was literally the washing of the brain, and was horrified imagining how that might be done - but now, that’s exactly what I need to do to purge the mental images evoked by the squelchy bits here, which seem to be adding bathtub flatulence to the porn menu. It’s perfectly decent electro-disco otherwise. Also, Tommy's hair is extraordinary.
Very pleasant.
Rambling Dylanesque lyrics. Musically basic.
I like more than my fair share of 80's hardcore punk, so I enjoyed the first half of the song.  The 2nd half is more in the style of Rage Against the Machine, which is less my thing.

Oh wow.  Not having much exposure to the music of the Arab world, I am not sure how this piece fits into the whole history of it and how to judge it.  It sounds much like the little I have heard; I don't know that it's a genre that values expansion and experimentation much?  So, just on an immediate aesthetic basis, I can say that I like the percussion, and the singer has a good voice.

I'm sorry for the loss of your valued friend, Ben.  Props to you for being publicly emotional regarding male friendship, a rare thing.
Gorgeous version of an already-gorgeous song, but not really very different from the original.  Would have gotten more points if the same album by Craig A hadn't already been used.

Smiths cosplay mixed with Them's "Gloria" or The Velvet Undergrounds' "Rock & Roll".
He has already been used twice, which is not acceptable.  Check the spreadsheet, noob.
If you had to choose her, you should have used a song that wasn't so overexposed.  Try to amaze and surprise us instead, noob.
Neat story.  A tasty little appetizer of a song.  Not a whole meal, though.  Reminds me a bit of King Crimson and/or Hawkwind.
Pretty good, for hippies.
Good guitar break, but the rest of it lacks something.
I like it.  Similar to a few other No Wave-y female-fronted bands of its time.  And I appreciate the obscurity of your selection.
It's alright.  Definitely perkier than the original.
Interesting stuff.  Thanks for being weird.
Kinda fun, but they were used too recently in season 7.
They do create a fine rumble.

They were used (in a delightfully surprising way) in season 7, and this song (which is admittedly good) has been overplayed on classic rock radio.  But thank you for not using Eric Carmen's awful solo work, which would be even worse.
I liked the intensity of this, though the last 2 minutes could have been profitably excised.
Excellent stuff.  Great deep dive.
Brilliantly cheesy Euro-disco.
Lovely song, the perfect way to kick off the firefly music league!
Nice groovy little number.
Rage against the machine vibes, I like it a lot and will explore more!
I don't get it, this sounds exactly like Paul Buchanan. Love this version of this song.
Don Lennon is someone I need to explore more, heard him in a previous music league I believe, and that song was equally catchy / interesting.
I love Sade, but have heard this so many times now, and though this was her big hit, she has way better songs IMHO.
Love Sonic Youth, but I didn't really click with this song..
Pleasant and interesting, love hearing about places like this.
Yeah, this is so so cool!
I love the Melbourne music scene, so many great bands, but I just didn't click with this song.
Great tune, love it when the synth comes in. Going straight on to my work out mix
A classic. takes me straight back to my young clubbing days
Sorry, might just be me, but I don't like this type a stuff
Not my cup of tea I'm afraid.
Very cool song
Nice

I love this kind of music so much, what a great discovery, thank you Poster! Cool female vocalist, energy, lyrics, music, guitar / reverb outro, perfect! This has gone straight into my "top music league songs" list.
Haha, Sheffield, love it! No seriously, I've visited once and it was delightful. Nice song.
Interesting, meant to be ironic & funny I guess. Enjoyable listen, though I doubt I'll ever listen to it again.
This very nice, espc. the string arrangement, but perhaps a little too indie MOR. You don't write the saddest song in the world by setting out to write the saddest song in the world.
Didn't even know this compatriot. This is good. There is an article about him in this week's Weekendavisen.
This is good punky stuff.
I like this, and am giving an extra point for stepping outside our indie comfort zone.
It's good, but I'm not bowled over.
Jingly jangly indie pop is a surefire way to win me over.
On one hand the familiarity tax beckons, on the other hand this is a classic pop song. What to do ... ? A single point will do.
It's not bad as such, just not quite up there with some of the others.
Not bad, but doesn't quite make the cut.
I'm no fan of the speakysinging, but this song elevates itself enough for me to like it.
Noise rock is always a difficult proposition. And in this instance it doesn't quite land for me.
I'm surprised The Chromatics haven't turned up until now! What's wrong with us?! I obviously like this.
You get an extra point for the audacity of submitting outright dance music, and such a classic one to boot.
There are a lot of better bands from Liverpool, but I'll take this! Gamelan gongish modernist compositions mixed with indie music.
This was glorious for a brief moment in time.
This is the kind of shouty singing I dislike. The song itself is kinda OK, but the shouting ...
I'm normally not big on downvoting repeats, but used in the last league is a bit to close.
Good indie rock with pop tendencies. Thumbs up!
It has got its moments, but I'm a little undecided on the final vote.
Sorry?
Quite a departure from his primary band, I love it.

I think they're perhaps a little overrated but it's still pretty cool that music this loud is garnering such widespread attention. I've seen them live at a festival early in the day a few years back, and will see them again at a festival later this year as the headliner. Those stories about the ex-guitarist and attempted murder are wild
Nothing wrong a with a little world music, welcome to Firefly ML !
Aw Ben, so sorry to hear about your friend Gordon. This song stands its own as well, I like the yearning here

So close to Saving without comments but then saw that many new folks didn't know the importance of the intro. 

This song is is pretty funny and charming, reminds me of Jens, and Pelle Carlberg especially

Where? When? How?? 
I don't know what to do, and the familiairty tax is prob going to be applied here
I love my summer job stories from the college years. How did so many obvisouly amazing things that nobody would want to hear about today actually happen in 3 year period?
Sounds like a lot of bands with female half spoken/half sung vocals, and yep, still a total sucker for it.

I actually loved this track when it started  but my dog thought the repetitive guitar notes were too much like the UPS truck alert so we had to stop listening quickly. Hopefully it didn't suck in the middle and end?
Nice deep pull, fellow Portlander!!
It's not you, it's Jesus Jones.
Straight 🔥

Dane on Dane controversy!! 

Fear not, I am available for peace talks June 22-26.
More of that post-punk goodness that I crave, but slightly diminished by my wish that I liked Mark Gouldthorpe's vocals better than I actually do.
I really like how Crowley structures the instrumentation... the song is like waves coming onto the shore. And Caroline is right, what an apt way to start the new league.
Not a big fan of when the vocals get all processed, but overall it's clever enough to sustain interest.

Having already voiced my enthusiasm in the chat, I am thrilled to see Turnstile here -- this was easily my favorite album from 2025, but I could never find the right opportunity to submit them in the league. I don't know that "Birds" is the most representative song, though, or maybe it's just that that I gravitate more to poppier tracks like "Never Enough" or "Seein' Stars".

Admittedly not something I would normally listen to, but I think it's fine as a representation. I'd like to hear more about your personal history with the song/artist, though. It also doesn't deserve a downvote, but I don't get to make the rules.

I haven't played Armstrong in years... as I recall, I was a bigger fan of As If To Nothing than this album. And in truth, I think I prefer the original Blue Nile version of this song, too. It's obviously still highly evocative of time and place, though. Raising a glass to you and Gordon.

See also Jonathan Richman and Loudon Wainwright III. I was going to say it doesn't really deserve the full snake eyes, but then I heard that "bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, bum, bum" part again.

Still a classic! Sade's vocals deserve every bit of attention, but over the years I've become a fan of Stuart Matthewman, too, and the Sweetback side project (he was also a big part of helping launch neo-soul singer Maxwell). I'm so used to the album version that the single edit here catches me off guard, LOL!
Found out Ivan passed away in 2008. RIP
This has grown on me nicely with repeated listens... reminds me in places of my beloved Rainer Ptacek. Intrigued enough to check out the entire album at some point.
Some well-placed Velvet Underground vibes for this New York vignette, complete with the mostly deadpan vocals. If you like this, check out the slightly more raucous "Davey Says" from the same album.
"Strangest and spikiest" is a good guide into this song. It's amazing how well the blueprint from this era still holds up over 40 years later.

Many of you probably already know this, but Chromatics were actually featured in the 2017 Twin Peaks revival, playing at The Bang Bang Bar (aka the Roadhouse) like something out of a Wim Wenders film. This remix, which I had not heard before now, is an interesting alternate version with a decidedly different energy.

In hindsight it's interesting that I was consuming these sounds more from UK artists like Jazzie B (Soul II Soul) and Nellie Hooper rather than the homegrown stuff, but I was at least aware of Kevin Saunderson. I also have fond memories of our friend group passing around cassettes of house music that someone had taped off the radio while visiting Chicago. Heady times.
As a Blue Man Group fan, I would ordinarily have more time for this kind of thing, but unfortunately the novelty wears off a bit...

Proof that Jesus Jones were so much more interesting than just "Right Here, Right Now"... there was a time when this would have been described as cyberpunk music. Certainly fit in my listening world alongside the likes of fellow sample-lovers Tackhead and Public Enemy.
Solid punk effort. Still not sure I can actually wrap my brain around the gap between this and "Feel It Still", LOL!
Such a pastiche of obvious influences (Pete Townshend, The Beatles, etc.), but it all comes together nicely and the guitars sound wonderful. The '70s were a different time, riff on.

Kind of a revved-up take on fellow Chicagoan Liz Phair, isn't it? Co-produced by Chris Walla, who has continued to do all kinds of good work after splitting from Death Cab for Cutie, but just seems a bit generic to my jaded ears.

"This song is the best song Giorgio Moroder never wrote." Can't really add much to that statement, although maybe we didn't really need to hear someone squeezing Seebach's lemon 'til the juice ran down his leg.

I think a strict interpretation of this week's brief renders my submission invalid, or at least dubious, but hopefully the Lee Marvin baritone and swelling strings are enough to pick up a few points. I have discovered this week that I like singing the "in the world" bits  in the moments immediately after waking  up when my voice is still somewhat gravelly.
Welcome Thomas, from a frequent watcher of your YouTube videos. I found this song pleasant if unremarkable.

I am reminded that I must watch Baltimorons, which has been on my list for  a while. I may even watch it today, now that I am retired, the only question is will it be before or after my golf lesson. These kids have Jane's Addiction records in their collection. I definitely hear Perry Farrell in the vocals. It was ok.
Nadeem picked a tough week in which to make his comeback. Not for me. Downvoted with apologies.

Sorry to hear about your friend, Ben. As a fellow frequent watcher of live football in a country where that's a niche interest, where people don't understand that community and connection are what it's about and only care about quality (this makes me roll my eyes so far back in my head, honestly), I understand and sympathise with your loss completely.  I prefer the original of this but, to coin a cliche, PB could sing the team sheets from the Rangers FC match programme and I would listen.
Welcome Keith. We've had Don Lennon before.  You'll find that this annoys some of the awkward types around here. I liked it then and I like this too.
I was surprised that I listened to this all the way through. It's pretty good isn't it?  The sax is really enjoyable.
What a great story... I enjoyed this even though it didn't really develop into anything.
Yeah this is pretty good. I'm reminded of Jake Xerxes Fussell in the singing,

Wait, what? You mean this isn't Dry Cleaning? It was good though which means I am in the unfortunate position of having to give points to Neil Jones who has almost certainly downvoted me just because with a damning "shittest song in the world more like".

I hear you Al! You'd think living in Dublin I'd be spoiled for choice but I found this round surprisingly tricky. Reminded me a bit of Siouxie and the banshees. This was a strong week so this gets a very harsh downvote with apologies.

This was very good and sounds great on these here swanky earbuds that the fam got me recently to celebrate the milestone birthday which welcomed me to my seventh decade on earth. As my sister said to me and I tend to agree - How can you be 60? I really enjoyed this.
Yeah loved this. Thanks for reminding me of it, you resident of a town in a Sufjan Stevens song you.

At times I thought I was enjoying this, at others I found it annoying. Downvotes are hard to come by this week but, on balance, I think I have to apply one, apologetically of course, here. Sorry Girvan.
I didn't care much for these in their, ahem, heyday and either it or my ears hasn't aged well in the meantime. Sorry Tony. Downvoted with apologies.
Really good. Mad juxtaposition between them and Portugal The Man. Their lyric, "I've been feeling it since 1966" is my mantra.
I liked the intro, didn't care much for the singing.  Lots of good stuff this week so the upvotes have to be spread thinly.
Pretty Catchy! I enjoyed this...
I'm not mad about this. Sorry Ant!
As soon as the do do do doos came in I was sold.
do like this although i would recommend adrian sets his sights a little higher and tries to write the best song in the world not just the saddest.
nice toe tappy groove.
had me fooled with the rumbling bass synth intro before exploding. love the energy.
this took me a few listens but very much grew on me despite ostensibly not being my "thing" at all. thank you for the cultural enrichment.
lovely back story, let your memories keep him ever present. very well known song and likely to get a lot of votes. i'm not really a blue nile evangelist although i know many are.
ok. little dry for me. bum bum bum.
too smooth an operation.
fantastic build and loved this even more as it develops.
not for me, sorry, dvwa.
did photos for them a while ago. like these very much. they don't actually sound like dry cleaning all the time.
(i guess) very much of it's time but this sparse determinedly independent sound is great.
nice chilled sound.
not for me, very well known and gleaming with too much eighties polish
i think i have to give this points for avoiding the obvious. and i like it very much but don't love it.
had these recently-ish. this is by far their best song, familiarity tax applies i'm afraid.
great stuff.
no thanks. sorry.
gets better as it goes on although does fall (a little - it's better than that) into the female fronted league standard submission trap.
better than i was expecting tbf and made me smile. is bubble sex a danish thing? please elucidate.
Would have preferred something a bit more cultured, but this is alright.
Some great lyrics, “the legumes they reek of meat”. Very good.
Slow start, does its thing, finishes. I like it.
I enjoyed this. Reading her Wikipedia it seems she’s an absolute megastar in the Middle East.
Lovely writeup, sorry about your friend. I still don’t get the Blue Nile though. I don’t think you’ll need my votes to do well.
Third appearance for Don. I’m not going to penalise for this, because I think it’s the only times I’ve heard him and I really like all three.

This is a good song, but I’m knocking points off because I’ve heard it a million times and you didn’t specify the town (I’m guessing London as I’m not aware of anybody in the group living in Nigeria).
Great story, but I’m not particularly enjoying the song.
This is nice, although I think you should have gone with Mother Root.
They are definitely a standout among the current crop of young Liverpool bands.
Alister, you had the whole history of bands from Melbourne to choose from, and this is what we get?
You can always get extra points for a Twin Peaks connection.
Very familiar, although I had no idea it was by Inner City.
I’ve driven through Bradford on Avon many times on the way to the End of the Road festival. Never knew it was the home of Jesus Jones. Unfortunately I never liked Jesus Jones.
This was not an enjoyable listen.
Excellent.
A friend suggested going to see Ratboys last week in Manchester. I’d never heard of them, had a listen and liked them. We didn’t go in the end. This is good, probably should have gone.
I can think of other Sheffield artists you could have considered, but this is very good.
Even without the sex noises, this is fantastic
New artist for me. Loved the guitars.  Great pick.

This was great!

Extra DV for awkward family interaction.
Pandering to the right people. Simply lovely.

I don't know how anyone could moan like that with the insistent piccolo-esque synth melody. If I had heard this as a young child it would've made me hide under my parents' bed with my hands over my ears. It's the same reaction I had to the horrid, early electronic "Popcorn Song".
Agree, this is excellent.
Excellent.  Like Victoria Willams crossed with Hammerbox. Love this.

The hook and structure deserve something,  but those lyrics, ick. I remember as a kid singing along to this. Realizing as an adult now what the subject matter, oy. Afternoon Delight gives me that same retro ick.
Have not heard Help before, thank you for the introduction!
Not really worth a downvote but its a tough rough. Apologies.
Very appealing. These are guitars they're banging on??
I actually remember dancing to this in a dance club in the 80s.
Really enjoying the amount of rocking female lead singers on this list.
Appealing.
Very nice, very Northwest coffee house.
Wish it were longer.
No write up? Assuming a newby. You get a point for joining us.
Sounds like a Rickenbacker to me. Sign me up.
Again, I' m a sucker for the strings, but are these synth strings? Lovely song.
Gorgeous. The antithesis of the previous song. Wow, the musicianship is fantastic. Love the addition of strings.
Not the usual sound I go for but appreciate the time signature changes. Found myself nodding my head.
Entertaining enough.
Feels like a porno starring James Bond. I think I might make this my walk up music, which would be the height of false advertising.
Although I had a bit of trouble finding the right mood for this one this week, it's a lovely tune.
The echoey parts were a bit jarring on the first couple listens, but this is cool. It sounds new and old at once.
I think I would have liked this once upon a time, but these days I need a little more chill. Sorry Mike... I do have fondness for the Baltimore area though!

I'm a little hesitant to call myself out, but It is a bit of stretch to my ears. I could attach that to the instrumentation and rhythms being very different from western music,  but I think it's as much that this music also feels communal and traditional in a very different way than my experience in the U.S. where individualism is unfortunately at the heart of everything. I'd like to connect with this more.
A lovely song and a lovely remembrance
This sounds too simple to be as enjoyable as it is. Totally fun on first and subsequent listens.

Classic, but I am at an age when this was an inescapable fixture on the radio. It still conjures core memories. I'm a hypocrite because my entry will not be new to anyone either, but this would get more points if it wasn't so familiar.
Although I found some elements to enjoy here, this is ultimately too cool, and too noisy for my lame ass.
A whole album of this might be a tall order for me, but I enjoyed the song.
Aptly described. This one steps just enough outside of usual radius to feel fresh.
I felt exactly the same about the wide open round, but you did well here. I am a little disappointed to miss out on Thrush and the Cunts though.
This is cool, and I agree 100% that this feels like prime soundtrack.
This round makes it feel personal to downvote, but it certainly isn't. I'm afraid there's just nothing here for me.
Lol, how did you know? Not entirely terrible, but I think one minute of this would have been more than enough.
No, this won't do.
Yikes! I don't disagree but yes, probably a good thing he is getting this out on stage.
This is a group the wife and I checked out recently (listening, not a show). Probably won't become a regular rotation for me, but I like it.
I'm not gonna play it before bedtime or anything, but this is how you do a pulsing beat without driving the listener crazy after awhile. Thanks for this one!
A not unpleasant racket, but I liked it less than most of the other stuff. The first underserved downvote of the league but probably not the last.

Culchie O'Hara comes bursting out of the tunnel with a mopey effort and a comment worthy of Eitzel himself. The song put me in mind of Bill Callahan more than ME. I note that the narrator doesn't write the saddest song, making it the indie miserables equivalent of that Tenacious D song about writing the greatest song in the world but then forgetting it.

'That's nice,' said my daughter sarcastically as she saw the title of this on the car display. However she was failing to appreciate the Dylan-goes-Goth quality of the lyrics. I should listen to more Iceage.
I liked this immediately and then each listen revealed another layer. A warm and welcome respite from indie shenanigans.

Sorry to hear about Gordon. I'd think this is a pretty wonderful send off for anyone. I've bought at least two Craig Armstrong albums for the Paul Buchanan content, but many other joys to be had within.

Top the heap this week, even if we have had him twice before (or three times, if you ask the search tool, which has him mixed up with some guy called John). It's snarky and yet kind of sympathetic as well - I mean, the debut album guy is annoying but I sort of admire his enthusiasm and would indeed buy his debut album.
Enjoyable enough to hear again, and I can possibly appreciate this a bit more than I did at the time when it was far too wine bar sophisticate for me, but it's also immensely familiar.

Love the Steve Shelley's dad story. I think the only dad of a rock star I've ever worked with is the father of James Cox from the very good Crows. Who actually don't sound that different to this very enjoyable ear-pounding.

I'm glad you went with the obscure (18 listeners) alternative to Modest Mouse. This one worked its way into my skull on repeat listens. I liked the chemistry between the two vocalists. Did not like their name sounding so much like Sun Kil Moon.
Thrush and the Cunts would have got all the points, obviously. This sounds like the worst summer ever. It's very good.

Fond memories of the original version appearing at the end of the first episode of The Return. I went on a bit of a Chromatics bender after that, but I don't remember hearing this version and I loved the little nods to the show in it (backwards sound effects and all).

My god, I've somehow got through life thinking Inner City were Londoners. I didn't;think even know this had been a hit internationally. Evokes big sixth-form nostalgia, but the points are going to have to go to stuff I don't know as well.

They actually were one of the first bands that came to mind when I was choosing. They're not just about hitting guitars with mallets, they also put aeolian hairs on the top of Bidston Observatory. One of them apparently has a day job as a receptionist at the University of Liverpool, which makes me wonder if he's ever given security access (or more likely refused it) to the submitter of this song.
I wonder how this sounds now if you're 15. Possibly like those records from the 50s about rocket travel. Anyway I was well into this at the time, though they sound a bit cringe now.

...unless of course you are Michael O'Hara doing a double bluff????

I wouldn't have guessed this was a 2024 record.  Admirably obscure, but ultimately not that interesting.
Should we be worried that this song about the virginal singer discovering the joy of sex opens with him sweatily grunting 'MAMA'? Different times, etc
Good stuff. Sounds like it's constantly about to fall apart any second for the first half, then sort of does, then isn't quite as interesting when it puts itself back together.

Is this really 1977? Sounds at least a couple of years ahead of the game if so. I've encountered these before in a different league, promised myself to investigate further, and then forgotten. I'll try to remember this time.
I want more bubble sex.
